danger_signals.visual_form: Undead and demonic enemy sprites with grotesque designs
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat; player must engage or avoid combat

danger_signals.visual_form: Dark, eerie level designs with gothic architecture and supernatural atmosphere
danger_signals.meaning: High-danger zone; progression toward evil stronghold

danger_signals.visual_form: Floating chest that can spawn an evil magician upon firing
danger_signals.meaning: Risk/reward mechanic; opening chests may debilitate Arthur (transform to elderly man or duck)

danger_signals.visual_form: Beams of light striking villagers (narrative framing)
danger_signals.meaning: World-state catastrophe; souls stolen by Lucifer's forces

safety_signals.visual_form: Item sprites dropped from defeated enemies or treasure chests
safety_signals.meaning: Resource availability; empowers Arthur for combat

safety_signals.visual_form: Gold armor and standard armor variants (especially in Master System version)
safety_signals.meaning: Increased resilience; player progression safety net

safety_signals.visual_form: Completion of levels 1-5 first cycle, then repeat with special weapon availability
safety_signals.meaning: Safe progression structure; game supports player advancement

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Princess Prin-Prin; knight protagonist Sir Arthur
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Village setting (initial narrative framing); castle as destination

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Lucifer (final boss); Beelzebub (boss checkpoint); evil magician minion
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Undead creatures, ghouls, demons with grotesque visual design
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Hellish castle; eerie, dark levels with gothic supernatural aesthetics

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Beams of light striking villagers; Princess Prin-Prin has her soul stolen in front of Arthur
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Arthur must quest through hellish levels to restore souls; passive villages become objective

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Six levels with standard enemy placement and weapon availability from chests/enemies
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Arthur progresses through levels 1-5, then loops back to level 1

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Special weapon appears in level loop; required to access Lucifer's chamber
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Arthur must equip special weapon and defeat Beelzebub before final door opens

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Hellish castle environment; demonic creatures populate all levels
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies swarm; player must combat or navigate through hostile territory

environmental_storytelling.example: Opening establishes soul theft event and Princess capture; world-state of catastrophe communicated through story
Levels escalate in hellish aesthetics; final destination is Lucifer's chamber in castle

environmental_storytelling.technique: Enemy type diversity as environmental narrative
environmental_storytelling.example: Undead and ghouls populate levels, indicating demonic invasion and supernatural occupation of kingdom

environmental_storytelling.technique: Treasure chest mechanic as world hazard
environmental_storytelling.example: Evil magician can inhabit chests, creating environmental risk that curses Arthur (transforms him)

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Advances through levels, fires weapons in multiple directions (including up/down mid-air), collects items
Reduced combat effectiveness or helplessness; movement/attack impaired until transformation wears or reverses
Engage Arthur in combat; spawn from level design; demonstrate supernatural threat
Must be defeated before final door to Lucifer opens; serves as progression checkpoint
Occupies chamber; represents end-state evil to be defeated; stealing souls manifest as final objective
Emerges from treasure chests; curses Arthur when encountered; demonstrates risk-reward of exploration

notes: Ghouls 'n Ghosts uses a dual-cycle structure (five levels played twice) to communicate progression and world-state escalation. Visual danger signals rely heavily on grotesque enemy design and hellish environments rather than explicit UI. Faction identity is simple: human protagonist vs. demonic legion occupying the world. The special weapon gating mechanism on second playthrough creates a world-state boundary that is behaviorally enforced. Transformation curse mechanic ties environmental hazards directly to visible player debilitation. Master System version adds power-up shop system not documented in arcade/Genesis baseline, suggesting potential world-state variation by platform. No explicit color-coded faction systems present; distinction relies on visual grotesqueness contrast.
